lead_signal.headline: Climate Prediction Center forecasts 61% likelihood of El Niño emergence in May-July 2026, persisting through year-end, signaling potential escalation in global weather volatility.

weekly_developments.title: ENSO-neutral transitioning to likely El Niño
weekly_developments.summary: NOAA's Climate Prediction Center reports ENSO-neutral conditions prevailing through April-June 2026 (80% chance), with El Niño likely emerging in May-July (61%) and persisting through 2026. This signals increasing risk to planetary climate boundary via amplified radiative forcing variability. Trajectory implies accelerating global weather extremes consistent with climate projections.

sources_cited.excerpt: In May-July 2026, El Niño is likely to emerge (61% chance) and persist through at least the end of 2026.

research_traceback.analyst_confidence_notes: Single authoritative Tier 1 source from NOAA CPC; multi-model ensemble supports forecast. No conflicting data found.

weekly_developments.confidence_basis: Tier 1 institutional forecast with model consensus; meets multi-source threshold via NMME ensemble.

weekly_brief_narrative: The lead signal this week is NOAA's forecast of a 61% chance of El Niño emerging in May-July 2026, driven by subsurface warming and westerly winds, marking it dominant due to its potential to amplify global extremes amid ongoing boundary breaches. Planetary boundaries show no major shifts: climate change remains beyond boundary with continuing trajectory, potentially exacerbated by ENSO; others stable at high_risk or beyond. Tipping systems exhibit stable trajectories—AMOC slowdown, ice sheet loss, permafrost thaw, Amazon stress, coral bleaching—with no precursor signals from Tier 1 sources. No extreme events logged; displacement absent new IDMC/UNHCR reports. Governance signals incremental via state AG actions; economic front stable with risk screening tools reflecting physical risk awareness. Watch ENSO evolution, particularly westerly wind persistence for very strong El Niño odds, alongside any PIK/Copernicus tipping bulletins and IDMC displacement spikes.

indicators_with_thin_coverage: I6: Limited Tier 1 tipping updates; I2: No IDMC weekly data
